serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (one glass), providing about 105 kcal and 25.9 g of carbohydrates.

nutrient_source.title: Honey juice drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Honey juice drink (CR ID D03070014). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Fruit Juice and Juice Drinks — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for fruit juice drink with honey
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 58 (medium) is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fruit juice drinks and honey-sweetened beverages. Honey has a published GI of approximately 55–58 (University of Sydney GI Database). Fruit juice drinks without fibre typically fall in the medium GI range (55–65). GL per serving of 6.0 is retained from the HPB source record, corresponding to a 100 ml reference portion (58 × 10.36 / 100 ≈ 6.0).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (8.4 g/100 ml) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite low sodium (5 mg/100 ml).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid honey juice drinks due to their high free-sugar content (8.4 g/100 ml), which is associated with elevated blood pressure independent of sodium intake; choose plain water or unsweetened beverages instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.4 g sugar/100 ml and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugars are absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial blood glucose spikes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Honey juice drinks are unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to their high free-sugar content (8.4 g/100 ml), absence of dietary fibre, and medium GI (58), which together produce rapid glycaemic excursions; whole fruit with intact fibre is strongly preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased cardiovascular disease risk through mechanisms including dyslipidaemia, insulin resistance, and elevated triglycerides.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit honey juice drinks, as habitual sugar-sweetened beverage intake (8.4 g sugar/100 ml) is linked to elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; unsweetened beverages are preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High free-sugar content (8.4 g/100 ml) with zero dietary fibre and no meaningful anti-inflammatory micronutrients at effective concentrations.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Honey juice drinks are not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as the high free-sugar content may promote systemic inflammation; whole fruit with intact fibre and antioxidants is preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey Juice Drink is a processed beverage made from fruit juice concentrate sweetened with honey, commonly consumed in Singapore and available as a packaged or freshly prepared drink. Known in Chinese as 蜂蜜果汁饮料, in Malay as Minuman jus madu, in Tamil as தேன் பழச்சாறு பானம், and in Hindi as शहद जूस पेय, it is a clear to lightly coloured liquid with a sweet, mildly fruity flavour and a smooth, watery texture. The drink contains no fat, no protein, and no dietary fibre, deriving virtually all of its energy (42 kcal/100 ml) from carbohydrates (10.36 g/100 ml), of which 8.4 g/100 ml is sugar. The sodium content is low at 5 mg/100 ml. Despite the natural origin of honey as a sweetener, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) because its free-sugar content exceeds 5 g/100 ml. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to rapid glycaemic absorption (GI 58, medium), and it raises cardiovascular and hypertension risk through habitual SSB consumption patterns. It contains no meaningful anti-inflammatory compounds at effective concentrations and is therefore not beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis management. It is vegetarian and gluten-free but not vegan due to the honey content. Typical serving size is 250 ml (one glass), providing approximately 105 kcal and 25.9 g of carbohydrates per serving.
common_user_queries: How many calories in honey juice drink?
Is honey juice drink high in sugar?
Is honey juice drink suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of honey juice drink?
Is honey juice drink gluten free?
蜂蜜果汁饮料 热量

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID D03070014) with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for honey and fruit juice drinks, which is the best available approved GI source for this food type. Local-language names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are standard translations of the English product description, consistent with Singapore multilingual naming conventions.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database; this beverage is a carbohydrate-based drink containing no protein by formulation. fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database; this beverage contains no fat by formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database; fruit juice concentrate without pulp contains no dietary fibre. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 per HPB source. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based beverage with no animal fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific honey juice drink formulation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. GI value of 58 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for honey (GI ~55–58) and fruit juice drinks; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in the approved GI database. GL of 6.0 per serving is retained from the HPB source record, corresponding to a 100 ml reference portion. This beverage is not vegan due to honey content. Dietary tag 'low_calorie' from input is retained as 42 kcal/100 ml is below the 70 kcal/100 ml threshold for low-calorie beverages. Multilingual names added in iteration 3 to satisfy G6: Chinese simplified (蜂蜜果汁饮料), Chinese traditional (蜂蜜果汁飲料), Malay (Minuman jus madu), Tamil (தேன் பழச்சாறு பானம்), Hindi (शहद जूस पेय). The record is catalogued under Singapore HPB Foodo and is treated as a Singapore-market beverage requiring full Singaporean multilingual coverage.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Added mandatory local-language names to local_names: chinese_simplified (蜂蜜果汁饮料), chinese_traditional (蜂蜜果汁飲料), malay (Minuman jus madu), tamil (தேன் பழச்சாறு பானம்), hindi (शहद जूस பेय). This resolves G6 hard gate failure from iteration 2.

Iteration 3: Added all local-language name strings to flat aliases array and to aliases_by_language.chinese, aliases_by_language.malay, aliases_by_language.tamil, aliases_by_language.hindi. This resolves Q1 and Q8 failures from iteration 2.
Iteration 3: Updated llm_training.natural_language_description to include multilingual name references for richer LLM/RAG retrieval.
Iteration 3: Updated confidence_reason and notes to document multilingual name additions and rationale for treating this HPB-catalogued beverage as requiring Singapore multilingual coverage.
